item.title: Re: P and ¬P
item.content:encoded: I don’t really think the principle of explosions really fits this premise. The logic for it basically is if a statement and the exact inverse of that statement are both true then logically any other statement is also true. But I don’t see the exact opposite state being true in this. Just two similar states taking place. He doesn’t simultaneously exist and not exist like a Schrödinger’s cat analogy he just exists twice. Which would be two different P statements

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This was pointed out to me soon after I posted, but due to JamCon restrictions I was unable to change it. I honestly can't be bothered to come up with an equivalent test now; if you really have to, pretend this exists in a universe where the DoD was a decade or so ahead of ours. Or hell, maybe the SCPF just signed a paratech contract with Pentagram to aid in location-based testing. As far as "the Foundation has weird advanced technology" goes, it's not exactly the biggest suspension of disbelief on the site.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Persons within SCP-5557 are capable of distorting baseline logical reality by extrapolating from this contradiction, in-line with the logical principle of explosion.

This is where you lost me. Or, more accurately, my comprehension. Which isn't a bad thing, I guess? Perhaps this is a deliberately meaningless sentence that's supposed to feel like you could parse it if you were a *bit* more clever. Or maybe I just don't get it.

Perhaps I'm being too damn picky here, but I feel like this should either commit to an understandable ruleset or an incomprehensible descent into chaos. Right now it teeters on an unsatisfying edge between the two—which is a perfectly reasonable place to end this sort of fiction, but in the broader context of the site and the mundanity of the non sequitur, it feels like an easy way out.

I am somehow literally never on the same wavelength as a MaliceAF skip. This one feels closer than most? There's something tantalizing here and it feels like something I could, at some point, piece together.

To quote an upvote on one of my old works, "Upvoted despite not having a bleeding clue what's going on."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The GPS project was started by the U.S. Department of Defense in 1973, with the first prototype spacecraft launched in 1978 and the full constellation of 24 satellites operational in 1993. Originally limited to use by the United States military, civilian use was allowed from the 1980s following an executive order from President Ronald Reagan[5]. Advances in technology and new demands on the existing system have now led to efforts to modernize the GPS and implement the next generation of GPS Block IIIA satellites and Next Generation Operational Control System (OCX).[6] Announcements from Vice President Al Gore and the White House in 1998 initiated these changes. In 2000, the U.S. Congress authorized the modernization effort, GPS III. During the 1990s, GPS quality was degraded by the United States government in a program called "Selective Availability"; this was discontinued in May 2000 by a law signed by President Bill Clinton.[7]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree with DrScaramouche and am doing the +1 fandango.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel simultaneously more smarter and more stupider for having read this article.

For some reason, this feels like the greatest compliment I've ever received on a work. Thank you :P

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I feel simultaneously more smarter and more stupider for having read this article.

2 simultaneous +1s.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Always love it when an article decides to not needlessly repeat examples of a property it has established and lunge for the gold in the experiment logs.

Wonderful conceptual chaos as always. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This encapsulates my thoughts exactly. I very much despised Symbolic Logic, but this was just batshit and wild from start to end. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
everybody gangsta til the footnote has a table in it

I'm not gonna act like I'm smart enough to fully understand this, but I do know that this was a wild ride, and one that I really enjoyed going on. This really squeezed my brain and that's a vibe that I like to get from articles.

item.title: P and ¬P
item.content:encoded:
This is my article for theme 1 of the 2020 Jamcon — it was "EXPLOSIONS", so I obviously went with the Principle of Explosion. Obviously. A complete explanation of this particular disjunctive delight can be found on Wikipedia.

The image is a composite of this and this, both from Wikimedia Commons.
